Location of Misasa Onsen }}
'''Misasa Onsen''' (三朝温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tōhaku District|en|Tōhaku District|WD=Q1131949|lt=Tōhaku District|1=Tōhaku District}}, {{ill|Misasa, Tottori|en|Misasa, Tottori|WD=Q1156186|lt=Misasa Town|1=Misasa, Tottori}}. It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|WD=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580978}}. It is one of the hot springs that constitute the {{ill|Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja|とっとり梨の花温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / Sodium {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|WD=Q11428612|lt=Chloride Hot Spring|1=Brine spring}} * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単純温泉}} They are Japan’s largest radon hot spring region, and radon hot springs with temperatures above 34°C are extremely rare worldwide.
Medical institutions implement hot spring therapy using these springs, which are utilized in treatments such as hot spring pool therapy, hot spring drinking therapy, and mineral mud therapy. Consequently, the hot springs are believed to exhibit significant hormesis effects, attracting therapeutic bathers as well as tourists.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Hotspring Yuda Yamaguchi 01.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A foot bath within {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park)]] The onsen town, located in the southwestern part of Yamaguchi City, features numerous accommodations including large hotels, traditional ryokan, and business hotels. Although few onsen towns exist along the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=Sanyō region|1=San'yō region}}, this is the premier onsen town on the Sanyō route. It also serves as a major lodging base for tourists visiting Yamaguchi City as well as for those heading to renowned sightseeing destinations such as {{ill|Hagi|en|Hagi|WD=Q413694|lt=Hagi City|1=Hagi}} and {{ill|Tsuwano|ja|津和野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuwano|1=Q11146437|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11146437}}, supported by an extensive bus network.
The area also functions as the central commercial and entertainment district of Yamaguchi City, offering many restaurants. Unlike onsen that emerge in the urban centers of prefectural capitals such as {{ill|Kōfu Onsen|ja|甲府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu Onsen|1=Kōfu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228449}}, {{ill|Tottori Onsen|ja|鳥取温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Onsen|1=Tottori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674516}}, {{ill|Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|ja|松江しんじ湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|1=Matsue-shinjiko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531630}}, and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dogo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}}, Misasa Onsen is located within a densely built urban area. Although it lacks the traditional atmosphere of a historic onsen town, several spots exist for nighttime strolls.
The Matsudaya Hotel in the onsen town is reputed as a lodging where patriots of the late Edo period once gathered. In addition, the hotel was selected by critic {{ill|Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|ja|石井宏子 (評論家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|1=Hiroko Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11584925}} as one of “10 Inns to Savor Gourmet Cuisine” and was also listed among “200 Recommended Inns & Communal Baths” by {{ill|Matsuda Tadanori|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Tadanori|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}}.
The town places strong emphasis on its foot baths, with five facilities including {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park). Since October 17, 2005, the Misasa Onsen Distribution Cooperative and {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|en|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|WD=Q3551848|lt=Yamaguchi Prefectural University|1=Yamaguchi Prefectural University}} have jointly conducted efficacy tests on these foot baths. In March 2015, Yamaguchi City opened a paid tourist circulation facility called “Fox’s Footprints.”
== Onsen Facilities == * Public Baths – Kawahara-yu (河原の湯) and Kabayu (株湯) * Foot Baths – Kawahara no Yu (河原の湯), Yakushi no Yu (薬師の湯), Kajika no Yu (かじかの湯), Kabayu (株湯) * Hot Spring Drinking Facilities – Yakushi no Yu, Kabayu, and the “Kami-no-Yu” at {{ill|Misasa Shrine|ja|三朝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} In addition, as a municipally operated national lodging facility in Misasa Town, “Brannal Misasa” was established. Its former name was Misasa Onsen Kaikan, which opened in 1963 (Shōwa 38) and was renamed Brannal Misasa in 1995 (Heisei 7). Brannal Misasa was transferred in 2023 (Reiwa 5) to a group company of G-Lion, the designated administrator. Furthermore, there was a communal bath called “Tamahari no Yu” that served both residents and visitors; however, due to aging facilities and an aging workforce, it ceased operations at the end of March 2023. The site of “Tamahari no Yu” is planned to be sold in May 2024 (Reiwa 6) to the G-Lion Group, which intends to construct a high-end ryokan there.
== Surrounding Areas == * {{ill|Mount Mitoku|en|Mount Mitoku|WD=Q6922220|lt=Santoku Mountain|1=Mount Mitoku}} (Mitoku-san) and {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}} – Along with the Nikkō Heritage Site “Land of Purification of the Six Roots and Healing of the Six Senses” (六根清浄と六感治癒の地) designated by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2015, featuring the {{ill|Tōnyūdō|ja|投入堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnyūdō|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} among others. * {{ill|Shikakan Kei|ja|小鹿渓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikakan Kei|1=Oshika Valley|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464496}} – A National Scenic Area. * {{ill|Uchibuki Tamagawa|ja|打吹玉川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchibuki Tamagawa|1=Utsubuki-tamagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497222}} – An Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District. * {{ill|Kurayoshi Park Square|ja|倉吉パークスクエア|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayoshi Park Square|1=Kurayoshi Park Square|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386555}} – Including the Kurayoshi Future Center and the Tottori 20th Century Pear Memorial Museum.
== Access == * Rail: Approximately 20–25 minutes by bus from {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|en|Kurayoshi Station|WD=Q899254|lt=Kurayoshi Station|1=Kurayoshi Station}} on the JR {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=San'in Main Line|1=San'in Main Line}} via {{ill|Hinonmaru Bus|ja|日ノ丸自動車|lt=Hinonmaru Bus|lt_ja=日ノ丸バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinonmaru Bus|1=Hinomaru Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504700}}. * Bus: Direct bus service for Misasa Onsen / Hawai Onsen. * By Car: Accessible via the {{ill|San'in Expressway|en|San'in Expressway|WD=Q5608657|lt=Sanyō Expressway|1=San'in Expressway}} from the Hawai Interchange via National Route 179 and Tottori Prefecture Road 21 (Tottori-Kanago-Kurayoshi Line); alternatively, from the {{ill|Yonago Expressway|en|Yonago Expressway|WD=Q11604113|lt=Yonago Expressway|1=Yonago Expressway}} via National Routes 313 and 482, National Route 179, and Tottori Prefecture Road 21. == References ==

It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|WD=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580978}}. Consequently, the hot springs are believed to exhibit significant hormesis effects, attracting therapeutic bathers as well as tourists.

Although few onsen towns exist along the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=Sanyō region|1=San'yō region}}, this is the premier onsen town on the Sanyō route. It also serves as a major lodging base for tourists visiting Yamaguchi City as well as for those heading to renowned sightseeing destinations such as {{ill|Hagi|en|Hagi|WD=Q413694|lt=Hagi City|1=Hagi}} and {{ill|Tsuwano|ja|津和野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuwano|1=Q11146437|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11146437}}, supported by an extensive bus network.

Unlike onsen that emerge in the urban centers of prefectural capitals such as {{ill|Kōfu Onsen|ja|甲府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu Onsen|1=Kōfu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228449}}, {{ill|Tottori Onsen|ja|鳥取温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Onsen|1=Tottori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674516}}, {{ill|Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|ja|松江しんじ湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|1=Matsue-shinjiko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531630}}, and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dogo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}}, Misasa Onsen is located within a densely built urban area. Although it lacks the traditional atmosphere of a historic onsen town, several spots exist for nighttime strolls.
The Matsudaya Hotel in the onsen town is reputed as a lodging where patriots of the late Edo period once gathered. In addition, the hotel was selected by critic {{ill|Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|ja|石井宏子 (評論家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|1=Hiroko Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11584925}} as one of “10 Inns to Savor Gourmet Cuisine” and was also listed among “200 Recommended Inns & Communal Baths” by {{ill|Matsuda Tadanori|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Tadanori|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}}.
The town places strong emphasis on its foot baths, with five facilities including {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park). Since October 17, 2005, the Misasa Onsen Distribution Cooperative and {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|en|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|WD=Q3551848|lt=Yamaguchi Prefectural University|1=Yamaguchi Prefectural University}} have jointly conducted efficacy tests on these foot baths. In March 2015, Yamaguchi City opened a paid tourist circulation facility called “Fox’s Footprints.”
== Onsen Facilities == * Public Baths – Kawahara-yu (河原の湯) and Kabayu (株湯) * Foot Baths – Kawahara no Yu (河原の湯), Yakushi no Yu (薬師の湯), Kajika no Yu (かじかの湯), Kabayu (株湯) * Hot Spring Drinking Facilities – Yakushi no Yu, Kabayu, and the “Kami-no-Yu” at {{ill|Misasa Shrine|ja|三朝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} In addition, as a municipally operated national lodging facility in Misasa Town, “Brannal Misasa” was established. Its former name was Misasa Onsen Kaikan, which opened in 1963 (Shōwa 38) and was renamed Brannal Misasa in 1995 (Heisei 7). Brannal Misasa was transferred in 2023 (Reiwa 5) to a group company of G-Lion, the designated administrator. Furthermore, there was a communal bath called “Tamahari no Yu” that served both residents and visitors; however, due to aging facilities and an aging workforce, it ceased operations at the end of March 2023. The site of “Tamahari no Yu” is planned to be sold in May 2024 (Reiwa 6) to the G-Lion Group, which intends to construct a high-end ryokan there.
== Surrounding Areas == * {{ill|Mount Mitoku|en|Mount Mitoku|WD=Q6922220|lt=Santoku Mountain|1=Mount Mitoku}} (Mitoku-san) and {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}} – Along with the Nikkō Heritage Site “Land of Purification of the Six Roots and Healing of the Six Senses” (六根清浄と六感治癒の地) designated by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2015, featuring the {{ill|Tōnyūdō|ja|投入堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnyūdō|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} among others. * {{ill|Shikakan Kei|ja|小鹿渓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikakan Kei|1=Oshika Valley|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464496}} – A National Scenic Area. * {{ill|Uchibuki Tamagawa|ja|打吹玉川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchibuki Tamagawa|1=Utsubuki-tamagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497222}} – An Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District. * {{ill|Kurayoshi Park Square|ja|倉吉パークスクエア|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayoshi Park Square|1=Kurayoshi Park Square|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386555}} – Including the Kurayoshi Future Center and the Tottori 20th Century Pear Memorial Museum.
== Access == * Rail: Approximately 20–25 minutes by bus from {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|en|Kurayoshi Station|WD=Q899254|lt=Kurayoshi Station|1=Kurayoshi Station}} on the JR {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=San'in Main Line|1=San'in Main Line}} via {{ill|Hinonmaru Bus|ja|日ノ丸自動車|lt=Hinonmaru Bus|lt_ja=日ノ丸バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinonmaru Bus|1=Hinomaru Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504700}}. * Bus: Direct bus service for Misasa Onsen / Hawai Onsen. * By Car: Accessible via the {{ill|San'in Expressway|en|San'in Expressway|WD=Q5608657|lt=Sanyō Expressway|1=San'in Expressway}} from the Hawai Interchange via National Route 179 and Tottori Prefecture Road 21 (Tottori-Kanago-Kurayoshi Line); alternatively, from the {{ill|Yonago Expressway|en|Yonago Expressway|WD=Q11604113|lt=Yonago Expressway|1=Yonago Expressway}} via National Routes 313 and 482, National Route 179, and Tottori Prefecture Road 21. == References ==
<references />

'''Misasa Onsen''' (三朝温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tōhaku District|en|Tōhaku District|WD=Q1131949|lt=Tōhaku District|1=Tōhaku District}}, {{ill|Misasa, Tottori|en|Misasa, Tottori|WD=Q1156186|lt=Misasa Town|1=Misasa, Tottori}}. It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|WD=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580978}}. It is one of the hot springs that constitute the {{ill|Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja|とっとり梨の花温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / Sodium {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|WD=Q11428612|lt=Chloride Hot Spring|1=Brine spring}} * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単純温泉}} They are Japan’s largest radon hot spring region, and radon hot springs with temperatures above 34°C are extremely rare worldwide.
Medical institutions implement hot spring therapy using these springs, which are utilized in treatments such as hot spring pool therapy, hot spring drinking therapy, and mineral mud therapy. Consequently, the hot springs are believed to exhibit significant hormesis effects, attracting therapeutic bathers as well as tourists.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Hotspring Yuda Yamaguchi 01.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A foot bath within {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park)]] The onsen town, located in the southwestern part of Yamaguchi City, features numerous accommodations including large hotels, traditional ryokan, and business hotels. Although few onsen towns exist along the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=Sanyō region|1=San'yō region}}, this is the premier onsen town on the Sanyō route. It also serves as a major lodging base for tourists visiting Yamaguchi City as well as for those heading to renowned sightseeing destinations such as {{ill|Hagi|en|Hagi|WD=Q413694|lt=Hagi City|1=Hagi}} and {{ill|Tsuwano|ja|津和野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuwano|1=Q11146437|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11146437}}, supported by an extensive bus network.
The area also functions as the central commercial and entertainment district of Yamaguchi City, offering many restaurants. Unlike onsen that emerge in the urban centers of prefectural capitals such as {{ill|Kōfu Onsen|ja|甲府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu Onsen|1=Kōfu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228449}}, {{ill|Tottori Onsen|ja|鳥取温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Onsen|1=Tottori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674516}}, {{ill|Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|ja|松江しんじ湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|1=Matsue-shinjiko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531630}}, and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dogo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}}, Misasa Onsen is located within a densely built urban area. Although it lacks the traditional atmosphere of a historic onsen town, several spots exist for nighttime strolls.
The Matsudaya Hotel in the onsen town is reputed as a lodging where patriots of the late Edo period once gathered. In addition, the hotel was selected by critic {{ill|Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|ja|石井宏子 (評論家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|1=Hiroko Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11584925}} as one of “10 Inns to Savor Gourmet Cuisine” and was also listed among “200 Recommended Inns & Communal Baths” by {{ill|Matsuda Tadanori|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Tadanori|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}}.
The town places strong emphasis on its foot baths, with five facilities including {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park). Since October 17, 2005, the Misasa Onsen Distribution Cooperative and {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|en|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|WD=Q3551848|lt=Yamaguchi Prefectural University|1=Yamaguchi Prefectural University}} have jointly conducted efficacy tests on these foot baths. In March 2015, Yamaguchi City opened a paid tourist circulation facility called “Fox’s Footprints.”
== Onsen Facilities == * Public Baths – Kawahara-yu (河原の湯) and Kabayu (株湯) * Foot Baths – Kawahara no Yu (河原の湯), Yakushi no Yu (薬師の湯), Kajika no Yu (かじかの湯), Kabayu (株湯) * Hot Spring Drinking Facilities – Yakushi no Yu, Kabayu, and the “Kami-no-Yu” at {{ill|Misasa Shrine|ja|三朝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} In addition, as a municipally operated national lodging facility in Misasa Town, “Brannal Misasa” was established. Its former name was Misasa Onsen Kaikan, which opened in 1963 (Shōwa 38) and was renamed Brannal Misasa in 1995 (Heisei 7). Brannal Misasa was transferred in 2023 (Reiwa 5) to a group company of G-Lion, the designated administrator. Furthermore, there was a communal bath called “Tamahari no Yu” that served both residents and visitors; however, due to aging facilities and an aging workforce, it ceased operations at the end of March 2023. The site of “Tamahari no Yu” is planned to be sold in May 2024 (Reiwa 6) to the G-Lion Group, which intends to construct a high-end ryokan there.
== Surrounding Areas == * {{ill|Mount Mitoku|en|Mount Mitoku|WD=Q6922220|lt=Santoku Mountain|1=Mount Mitoku}} (Mitoku-san) and {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}} – Along with the Nikkō Heritage Site “Land of Purification of the Six Roots and Healing of the Six Senses” (六根清浄と六感治癒の地) designated by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2015, featuring the {{ill|Tōnyūdō|ja|投入堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnyūdō|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} among others. * {{ill|Shikakan Kei|ja|小鹿渓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikakan Kei|1=Oshika Valley|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464496}} – A National Scenic Area. * {{ill|Uchibuki Tamagawa|ja|打吹玉川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchibuki Tamagawa|1=Utsubuki-tamagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497222}} – An Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District. * {{ill|Kurayoshi Park Square|ja|倉吉パークスクエア|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayoshi Park Square|1=Kurayoshi Park Square|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386555}} – Including the Kurayoshi Future Center and the Tottori 20th Century Pear Memorial Museum.
== Access == * Rail: Approximately 20–25 minutes by bus from {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|en|Kurayoshi Station|WD=Q899254|lt=Kurayoshi Station|1=Kurayoshi Station}} on the JR {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=San'in Main Line|1=San'in Main Line}} via {{ill|Hinonmaru Bus|ja|日ノ丸自動車|lt=Hinonmaru Bus|lt_ja=日ノ丸バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinonmaru Bus|1=Hinomaru Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504700}}. * Bus: Direct bus service for Misasa Onsen / Hawai Onsen. * By Car: Accessible via the {{ill|San'in Expressway|en|San'in Expressway|WD=Q5608657|lt=Sanyō Expressway|1=San'in Expressway}} from the Hawai Interchange via National Route 179 and Tottori Prefecture Road 21 (Tottori-Kanago-Kurayoshi Line); alternatively, from the {{ill|Yonago Expressway|en|Yonago Expressway|WD=Q11604113|lt=Yonago Expressway|1=Yonago Expressway}} via National Routes 313 and 482, National Route 179, and Tottori Prefecture Road 21. == References ==

'''Misasa Onsen''' (三朝温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tōhaku District|en|Tōhaku District|WD=Q1131949|lt=Tōhaku District|1=Tōhaku District}}, {{ill|Misasa, Tottori|en|Misasa, Tottori|WD=Q1156186|lt=Misasa Town|1=Misasa, Tottori}}. It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|WD=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580978}}. It is one of the hot springs that constitute the {{ill|Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja|とっとり梨の花温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / Sodium {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|WD=Q11428612|lt=Chloride Hot Spring|1=Brine spring}} * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単純温泉}} They are Japan’s largest radon hot spring region, and radon hot springs with temperatures above 34°C are extremely rare worldwide.
Medical institutions implement hot spring therapy using these springs, which are utilized in treatments such as hot spring pool therapy, hot spring drinking therapy, and mineral mud therapy. Consequently, the hot springs are believed to exhibit significant hormesis effects, attracting therapeutic bathers as well as tourists.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Hotspring Yuda Yamaguchi 01.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A foot bath within {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park)]] The onsen town, located in the southwestern part of Yamaguchi City, features numerous accommodations including large hotels, traditional ryokan, and business hotels. Although few onsen towns exist along the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=Sanyō region|1=San'yō region}}, this is the premier onsen town on the Sanyō route. It also serves as a major lodging base for tourists visiting Yamaguchi City as well as for those heading to renowned sightseeing destinations such as {{ill|Hagi|en|Hagi|WD=Q413694|lt=Hagi City|1=Hagi}} and {{ill|Tsuwano|ja|津和野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuwano|1=Q11146437|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11146437}}, supported by an extensive bus network.
The area also functions as the central commercial and entertainment district of Yamaguchi City, offering many restaurants. Unlike onsen that emerge in the urban centers of prefectural capitals such as {{ill|Kōfu Onsen|ja|甲府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu Onsen|1=Kōfu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228449}}, {{ill|Tottori Onsen|ja|鳥取温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Onsen|1=Tottori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674516}}, {{ill|Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|ja|松江しんじ湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|1=Matsue-shinjiko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531630}}, and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dogo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}}, Misasa Onsen is located within a densely built urban area. Although it lacks the traditional atmosphere of a historic onsen town, several spots exist for nighttime strolls.
The Matsudaya Hotel in the onsen town is reputed as a lodging where patriots of the late Edo period once gathered. In addition, the hotel was selected by critic {{ill|Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|ja|石井宏子 (評論家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|1=Hiroko Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11584925}} as one of “10 Inns to Savor Gourmet Cuisine” and was also listed among “200 Recommended Inns & Communal Baths” by {{ill|Matsuda Tadanori|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Tadanori|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532059}}.
The town places strong emphasis on its foot baths, with five facilities including {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park). Since October 17, 2005, the Misasa Onsen Distribution Cooperative and {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|en|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|WD=Q3551848|lt=Yamaguchi Prefectural University|1=Yamaguchi Prefectural University}} have jointly conducted efficacy tests on these foot baths. In March 2015, Yamaguchi City opened a paid tourist circulation facility called “Fox’s Footprints.”
== Onsen Facilities == * Public Baths – Kawahara-yu (河原の湯) and Kabayu (株湯) * Foot Baths – Kawahara no Yu (河原の湯), Yakushi no Yu (薬師の湯), Kajika no Yu (かじかの湯), Kabayu (株湯) * Hot Spring Drinking Facilities – Yakushi no Yu, Kabayu, and the “Kami-no-Yu” at {{ill|Misasa Shrine|ja|三朝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} In addition, as a municipally operated national lodging facility in Misasa Town, “Brannal Misasa” was established. Its former name was Misasa Onsen Kaikan, which opened in 1963 (Shōwa 38) and was renamed Brannal Misasa in 1995 (Heisei 7). Brannal Misasa was transferred in 2023 (Reiwa 5) to a group company of G-Lion, the designated administrator. Furthermore, there was a communal bath called “Tamahari no Yu” that served both residents and visitors; however, due to aging facilities and an aging workforce, it ceased operations at the end of March 2023. The site of “Tamahari no Yu” is planned to be sold in May 2024 (Reiwa 6) to the G-Lion Group, which intends to construct a high-end ryokan there.
== Surrounding Areas == * {{ill|Mount Mitoku|en|Mount Mitoku|WD=Q6922220|lt=Santoku Mountain|1=Mount Mitoku}} (Mitoku-san) and {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}} – Along with the Nikkō Heritage Site “Land of Purification of the Six Roots and Healing of the Six Senses” (六根清浄と六感治癒の地) designated by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2015, featuring the {{ill|Tōnyūdō|ja|投入堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnyūdō|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} among others. * {{ill|Shikakan Kei|ja|小鹿渓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikakan Kei|1=Oshika Valley|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464496}} – A National Scenic Area. * {{ill|Uchibuki Tamagawa|ja|打吹玉川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchibuki Tamagawa|1=Utsubuki-tamagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497222}} – An Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District. * {{ill|Kurayoshi Park Square|ja|倉吉パークスクエア|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayoshi Park Square|1=Kurayoshi Park Square|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386555}} – Including the Kurayoshi Future Center and the Tottori 20th Century Pear Memorial Museum.
== Access == * Rail: Approximately 20–25 minutes by bus from {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|en|Kurayoshi Station|WD=Q899254|lt=Kurayoshi Station|1=Kurayoshi Station}} on the JR {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|WD=Q1135170|lt=San'in Main Line|1=San'in Main Line}} via {{ill|Hinonmaru Bus|ja|日ノ丸自動車|lt=Hinonmaru Bus|lt_ja=日ノ丸バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinonmaru Bus|1=Hinomaru Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11504700}}. * Bus: Direct bus service for Misasa Onsen / Hawai Onsen. * By Car: Accessible via the {{ill|San'in Expressway|en|San'in Expressway|WD=Q5608657|lt=Sanyō Expressway|1=San'in Expressway}} from the Hawai Interchange via National Route 179 and Tottori Prefecture Road 21 (Tottori-Kanago-Kurayoshi Line); alternatively, from the {{ill|Yonago Expressway|en|Yonago Expressway|WD=Q11604113|lt=Yonago Expressway|1=Yonago Expressway}} via National Routes 313 and 482, National Route 179, and Tottori Prefecture Road 21. == References ==

'''Misasa Onsen''' (三朝温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tōhaku District|en|Tōhaku District|qid=Q1131949|lt=Tōhaku District|1=Tōhaku District}}, {{ill|Misasa, Tottori|en|Misasa, Tottori|qid=Q1156186|lt=Misasa Town|1=Misasa, Tottori}}. It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|qid=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580978}}. It is one of the hot springs that constitute the {{ill|Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja|とっとり梨の花温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

'''Misasa Onsen''' (三朝温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tōhaku District|en|Tōhaku District|qid=Q1131949|lt=Tōhaku District|1=Tōhaku District}}, {{ill|Misasa, Tottori|en|Misasa, Tottori|qid=Q1156186|lt=Misasa Town|1=Misasa, Tottori}}. It is recognized as one of {{ill|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|en|100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)|qid=Q2806722|lt=Japan's 100 Views|1=100 Landscapes of Japan (Shōwa era)}}.
As of 2017, the annual number of bathers Ranks second in Tottori Prefecture, following {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580978}}. It is one of the hot springs that constitute the {{ill|Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja|とっとり梨の花温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Nashinohana Onsen Resort|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / Sodium {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|qid=Q11428612|lt=Chloride Hot Spring|1=Brine spring}} * {{ill|Radioactive hot spring|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|1=Radioactive spring|lt=Radon-containing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Radioactive spring|qq=}} / {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単純温泉}} They are Japan’s largest radon hot spring region, and radon hot springs with temperatures above 34°C are extremely rare worldwide.
Medical institutions implement hot spring therapy using these springs, which are utilized in treatments such as hot spring pool therapy, hot spring drinking therapy, and mineral mud therapy. Consequently, the hot springs are believed to exhibit significant hormesis effects, attracting therapeutic bathers as well as tourists.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Hotspring Yuda Yamaguchi 01.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A foot bath within {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park)]] The onsen town, located in the southwestern part of Yamaguchi City, features numerous accommodations including large hotels, traditional ryokan, and business hotels. Although few onsen towns exist along the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|qid=Q398087|lt=Sanyō region|1=San'yō region}}, this is the premier onsen town on the Sanyō route. It also serves as a major lodging base for tourists visiting Yamaguchi City as well as for those heading to renowned sightseeing destinations such as {{ill|Hagi|en|Hagi|qid=Q413694|lt=Hagi City|1=Hagi}} and {{ill|Tsuwano|ja|津和野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuwano|1=Q11146437|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11146437}}, supported by an extensive bus network.
The area also functions as the central commercial and entertainment district of Yamaguchi City, offering many restaurants. Unlike onsen that emerge in the urban centers of prefectural capitals such as {{ill|Kōfu Onsen|ja|甲府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu Onsen|1=Kōfu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17228449}}, {{ill|Tottori Onsen|ja|鳥取温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Onsen|1=Tottori Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11674516}}, {{ill|Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|ja|松江しんじ湖温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen|1=Matsue-shinjiko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11531630}}, and {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|qid=Q1037748|lt=Dogo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}}, Misasa Onsen is located within a densely built urban area. Although it lacks the traditional atmosphere of a historic onsen town, several spots exist for nighttime strolls.
The Matsudaya Hotel in the onsen town is reputed as a lodging where patriots of the late Edo period once gathered. In addition, the hotel was selected by critic {{ill|Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|ja|石井宏子 (評論家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Hiroko (Critic)|1=Hiroko Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11584925}} as one of “10 Inns to Savor Gourmet Cuisine” and was also listed among “200 Recommended Inns & Communal Baths” by {{ill|Matsuda Tadanori|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Tadanori|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11532059}}.
The town places strong emphasis on its foot baths, with five facilities including {{ill|Inoue Park|ja|井上公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Park|1=Inoue Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16277560}} (formerly Takada Park). Since October 17, 2005, the Misasa Onsen Distribution Cooperative and {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|en|Yamaguchi Prefectural University|qid=Q3551848|lt=Yamaguchi Prefectural University|1=Yamaguchi Prefectural University}} have jointly conducted efficacy tests on these foot baths. In March 2015, Yamaguchi City opened a paid tourist circulation facility called “Fox’s Footprints.”
== Onsen Facilities == * Public Baths – Kawahara-yu (河原の湯) and Kabayu (株湯) * Foot Baths – Kawahara no Yu (河原の湯), Yakushi no Yu (薬師の湯), Kajika no Yu (かじかの湯), Kabayu (株湯) * Hot Spring Drinking Facilities – Yakushi no Yu, Kabayu, and the “Kami-no-Yu” at {{ill|Misasa Shrine|ja|三朝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} In addition, as a municipally operated national lodging facility in Misasa Town, “Brannal Misasa” was established. Its former name was Misasa Onsen Kaikan, which opened in 1963 (Shōwa 38) and was renamed Brannal Misasa in 1995 (Heisei 7). Brannal Misasa was transferred in 2023 (Reiwa 5) to a group company of G-Lion, the designated administrator. Furthermore, there was a communal bath called “Tamahari no Yu” that served both residents and visitors; however, due to aging facilities and an aging workforce, it ceased operations at the end of March 2023. The site of “Tamahari no Yu” is planned to be sold in May 2024 (Reiwa 6) to the G-Lion Group, which intends to construct a high-end ryokan there.
== Surrounding Areas == * {{ill|Mount Mitoku|en|Mount Mitoku|qid=Q6922220|lt=Santoku Mountain|1=Mount Mitoku}} (Mitoku-san) and {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3471689}} – Along with the Nikkō Heritage Site “Land of Purification of the Six Roots and Healing of the Six Senses” (六根清浄と六感治癒の地) designated by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2015, featuring the {{ill|Tōnyūdō|ja|投入堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnyūdō|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q19826408}} among others. * {{ill|Shikakan Kei|ja|小鹿渓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikakan Kei|1=Oshika Valley|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11464496}} – A National Scenic Area. * {{ill|Uchibuki Tamagawa|ja|打吹玉川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchibuki Tamagawa|1=Utsubuki-tamagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11497222}} – An Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District. * {{ill|Kurayoshi Park Square|ja|倉吉パークスクエア|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayoshi Park Square|1=Kurayoshi Park Square|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11386555}} – Including the Kurayoshi Future Center and the Tottori 20th Century Pear Memorial Museum.
== Access == * Rail: Approximately 20–25 minutes by bus from {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|en|Kurayoshi Station|qid=Q899254|lt=Kurayoshi Station|1=Kurayoshi Station}} on the JR {{ill|San'in Main Line|en|San'in Main Line|qid=Q1135170|lt=San'in Main Line|1=San'in Main Line}} via {{ill|Hinonmaru Bus|ja|日ノ丸自動車|lt=Hinonmaru Bus|lt_ja=日ノ丸バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinonmaru Bus|1=Hinomaru Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504700}}. * Bus: Direct bus service for Misasa Onsen / Hawai Onsen. * By Car: Accessible via the {{ill|San'in Expressway|en|San'in Expressway|qid=Q5608657|lt=Sanyō Expressway|1=San'in Expressway}} from the Hawai Interchange via National Route 179 and Tottori Prefecture Road 21 (Tottori-Kanago-Kurayoshi Line); alternatively, from the {{ill|Yonago Expressway|en|Yonago Expressway|qid=Q11604113|lt=Yonago Expressway|1=Yonago Expressway}} via National Routes 313 and 482, National Route 179, and Tottori Prefecture Road 21. == References ==
